ks1.objective: Dribble through the gates at top speed without bumping into anyone.
successCriteria: Picks a gate and goes for it
Tries again straight away after losing the ball

lks2.objective: Dribble bravely through gates at top speed against passive defenders — react positively to losing the ball.
successCriteria: Doesn't avoid defenders — tries to beat them
Recovers the ball quickly after losing it

uks2.objective: Express skill on the ball under pressure — pick the right gate, beat defenders, recover quickly when possession is lost.
successCriteria: Uses turns and skills to evade
Scans for the next gate before arriving
Body language stays positive after mistakes

space.description: Square area with gates set up inside as shown in the FFF card layout. Each child starts inside the area with a football.

value.name: Positivity
value.text: Being brave on the ball doesn't mean everything will work every time. Positivity is about having a go, accepting mistakes, and trying again with confidence. The bravest players stay positive when things don't go their way — every attempt helps you learn.

steps: Each child has a football. Dribble around the area and through the gates.
Encourage top-speed dribbling — different parts of the foot, both feet if able.
After warming up, introduce passive defenders who dribble their own ball and try to block attackers at the gates.
When children lose possession, react positively — get back on the ball quickly and try again.
progressions: Begin without defenders — build confidence and rhythm.
Add passive defenders (they dribble their own ball, block but don't tackle).
Defenders can now win the ball.
Immediately re-try after losing possession — reinforce the positive reaction.
questions: What do you do straight after losing the ball?
How can you tell which gate to go for?
What does 'being brave' mean on the ball?

step.space: Reduce area to increase pressure and decision-making
step.task: Use weaker foot when travelling or escaping pressure
step.equipment: Make the size of the gates smaller
step.people: Increase the number of defenders — including some who can tackle between gates

base.showmanship: Use moments where children lose the ball as opportunities to reinforce positivity. Highlight quick reactions, positive body language and willingness to try again. Make it clear that mistakes are expected and valued as part of learning.
